dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for a possible seizure at a second-story apartment in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Maria, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what happened today?
She had a possible seizure, and since then has been experiencing chest, stomach, and jaw pain.
Can you tell me your name, ma'am? What is today's date for you?
My name is Lin. It is June, but I am not sure which day it is.
Do you know where we are?
We are in an apartment. I don't know the address exactly, but I am in my room.
We're going to check your airway. Can you take a deep breath?
Okay.
Resp: 22. SpO2: 99% on room air. Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
Pulse - 126 and regular. BP is 113/65. Skin is pale, clammy, and wet. Radial pulses are strong.
Skin appears pale, slightly diaphoretic.
Pulse is 126, regular rhythm, measured at the radial artery.
Respiratory rate is 22 and regular.
O2 saturation is 99% while breathing room air.
Blood pressure is 113/65 with a seated position.
Glucose is 118 mg/dL. The finger stick was painless.
EKG reveals sinus tachycardia. No acute ST changes observed.
We're going to attempt an IV to monitor if it's okay with you.
Can you tell me if you have allergies, medications, or any medical history I should know about?
She says she is 18, weighs about 140 pounds, and only speaks limited English.
From what she said, she has no known medical history. She just moved here and hasn't seen a doctor in a while.
On a scale where 0 is no pain and 10 is unbearable, how bad is your pain?
It is around a 4. It's in my chest, belly, back, and jaw at the same time.
The patient's skin is pale and damp. Some old scars are visible on her belly but nothing new.
I will now examine your head and neck. Do you have any pain or numbness in this area?
No clear drainage or discharges. Mouth and ears appear clear.
Lung sounds are clear, no adventitious sounds. The patient states chest discomfort.
Based on field findings, we are following protocol 2-1 for chest pain with cardiac concern. Transport to Chippenham.
Vitals rechecked at 22:30. Pulse is 118, respiratory rate 22, BP 119/83. Skin is still pale.
IV start was unsuccessful, so we'll continue monitoring.
Two 12-lead ECGs were done. Both show sinus tach with nonspecific T wave changes.
We'll transport you to Chippenham Hospital.
Patient is securely placed in semi-Fowler's position using a five-point harness.
Patient is transported cold due to failed IV but remains closely monitored.

For Chippenham ED, we have an 18-year-old female with chest pain. Vitals at 22:30 include P:118, RR:22, BP:119/83, SpO2:99. EKG: sinus tach with nonspecific T wave changes. Two 12-lead ECGs done. Old abdominal scars present. Pain in chest, belly, back, jaw. IV line attempted, unsuccessful, now being monitored. She is anxious, speaks limited English, no ID or VISA. Emergency contacts given to ED.
Thank you, Chippenham crew. We are over.
Patient appears anxious and was initially reluctant to answer questions. Seems she may be dealing with anxiety.
She was not very talkative at first, but calmed down when we got her in the ambulance.
She was able to move all limbs. No weakness or paralysis noted.
Mental status appears normal, but she showed signs of high anxiety and stress.
Abdomen appears normal at rest except for old scars and some tenderness in the midsection.
She denies medications or allergies. Claims no medical history.
The patient made a grunting sound upon our arrival. It stopped after a few minutes.
IV attempt made at 22:11 was unsuccessful, no further attempts made.
Patient is secured for transport without visible injuries.
She appears pale and clammy with anxious expression.
No medications administered. Focused on monitoring and IV setup.
Patient was transported cold due to IV failure. Otherwise, stable.
She is an 18-year-old immigrant with no known medical insurance. No VISA or ID present.
Alert but visibly anxious. GCS 15 with appropriate verbal and motor responses.
No signs of impaired swallowing or swallowing discomfort noted.
No signs of discharge or nasal congestion. Throat is not swollen or inflamed.
No signs of jaundice, rashes, or new bites. Hydration not assessed due to time constraints.
Transportation to Chippenham due to concern for cardiac issue and field EKG findings.
Patient is in a semi-Fowler's position to assist with breathing and comfort.
Five-point harness used for safety during transport.
Two 12-lead EKGs performed in the field confirmed sinus tach with nonspecific T wave changes.
We are following Protocol 2-1 due to elevation of the concern for cardiac condition.
IV site was flushed during transport. No additional procedures performed.
She continues to report no significant medical history and no new symptoms.
Pain remains at a 4. No new locations noted.
Scars are visible but not new. No other changes to skin noted.
No adventitious sounds noted. Chest discomfort still reported.
Five-point harness maintained throughout.
Patient stable and alert during transport. No signs of increased distress.
No deformities, ligaments intact. Patient moves all limb parts appropriately.
Patient denies genitourinary concerns.
Standard five-point harness used without incident.
Confirmation provided to receive patient at Chippenham ED Bed 16 at 22:48.
Chippenham chose due to relevance to the suspected cardiac protocol.
The semi-Fowler's position was used for breathing assistance and comfort.
Limited communication due to language barriers, but able to follow simple instructions and report pain areas.
Vitals were rechecked at 22:30 and are consistent with initial findings.
She is cooperative once in the ambulance, though anxious at moments.
Destination is Chippenham, selected due to symptom nature and findings.
All data and resources properly handed over to the receiving ED team.
For the nursing staff at Chippenham, emergency contact details were provided.
The patient was handed over to the nurse team at their bedside with no delays noted.
No medications administered. Only IV monitoring and EKG procedures were performed in the field.
Transport duration within the expected range for this case complexity.
Patient was in stable condition during transport, with stable vitals and chest discomfort.
All safety steps taken ensured safe arrival to Chippenham ED.
Final checks performed. No further actions required before arrival.
Chippenham was notified directly by dispatch and has confirmed receipt.
All patient documents and updated EMT notes were given at the time of transfer.
We are ready to assist ED with any needed follow-up or additional information.
All reports were delivered in a calm and clear manner to facilitate ED response.
Simple language used due to limited English comprehension to keep communication clear.
Real-time documentation and updates were transmitted with confirmation received at Chippenham.
